title: Chamber over the hall as improved in 1768
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. 1768 mantel, second-floor chamber over the hall.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Curtail step, 1840s
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Catch for latch to Scotch oven; 1765
headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. title: Water table detail, ca. 1740
headline: Colonel Philip Lightfoot House, 302 Francis Street East, Williamsburg, Virginia
description: Townhouse of William Lightfoot.  William Byrd visited him here in June of 1740, both being on the Governor's Council at the time. Philip Lightfoot was one of the wealthiest planters of Virginia, owning plantations in York, Charles City, Surry, Goochland, Brunswick, New Kent and Hanover Counties. He was a merchant, acted as an attorney. The Williamsburg building served as his townhouse, as he  held residences in Yorktown and his manor-plantation house at Sandy Point, Charles City County. Presumably the house was built not long before 1740.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Glazed header Flemish bond rear, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Built late 18th c; modiied in the 1830s; tilted false plate
headline: Sotterley Corn Crib, 44300 Sotterley Lane, Hollywood, Maryland UTM: 38.376470, -76.542078
description: Timber framed corn crib probably built in the late 18th century fashioned out of an earlier building parts and then remodeled in the 1830s or 40s. Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Back gable wall sheathed and covered with thin, wooden shingles, recently replaced with new shingles. Tilited false plate; post bays; sidewall assembly. Hewn oak rafters set flat. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.

title: Kitchen brick detail, ca. 1750
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: kitchen,brick,masonry,flemish bond,glazed header flemish bond,joint,ruled joint,glaze,glazing

title: Doorway and remains of Inca stonework in village (2400s)
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Queen post truss, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Original kitchen and stairs; 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Alleway in village
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Schifferstadt House, 1110 Rosemont Avenue, Frederick, Maryland

First-story kitchen inldues a window with a sink to discard water through it. Stoves on upper floor  fed through arches in the passage. German principal rafter roof with  stacked common rafters. 1858 brick addition. Original stove marked with the date 1758; second-floor chamber over the kitchen

title: Front elevation, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: house,dwelling,brick,masonry,glazed header flemish bond,flemish bond,dormer,balcony,window,cellar window

The two end wings were raised to two stories in teh early 19th century, probably the 1810s. Owned by the Harrison from during the 1th century and until 1926. Restored by Robert Daniel in the 1920s. Second floor bedchamber in central block

title: Built late 18th c; modiied in the 1830s; attic access for wagon
headline: Sotterley Corn Crib, 44300 Sotterley Lane, Hollywood, Maryland UTM: 38.376470, -76.542078
description: Timber framed corn crib probably built in the late 18th century fashioned out of an earlier building parts and then remodeled in the 1830s or 40s. Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Back gable wall sheathed and covered with thin, wooden shingles, recently replaced with new shingles. Tilited false plate; post bays; sidewall assembly. Hewn oak rafters set flat. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. title: Copy of 1850s plat
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Strut to principal rafter joinery; 1804
headline: Rock Run, 4389 Rock Run Road, Havre de Grace, Harford County, Maryland
description: House built 1804 (date brick in gable). Two story, double pile with rear wing, the back of which was used as kitchen on the first floor, quarters on the second and attic floors, and for wet storage in the cellar. Note holes in sheathing possible for brackets on roof to hold slates while roofing (slater's bracket). This picture shows the strut which rises from the king post at the point where it joins the principal rafter. Note that the lower peg near this joint was intended to be pegged to hold the joint tight but the peg was never installed. Pegs along the top of the principal rafter are for an unknown purpose, perhaps suggesting the rafter was made from a reused timber. Note also the split-off end of the board made after the log from which it was fashioned was sawn at a sash mill. That board also has a lumber mark inscribed on its top face XXII.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Home house quarter, ca. 1850
headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Two room quarter but perhaps originally with only one door. Tenon and pinned rafters, originally with collars/ Used as a tenant house into the 20th century.

title: Mantel detail in inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Header course over original door (later infilled) in expanded ki
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Doorway and remains of Inca stonework in village (2400s)
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

Philip Lightfoot was one of the wealthiest planters of Virginia, owning plantations in York, Charles City, Surry, Goochland, Brunswick, New Kent and Hanover Counties. He was a merchant, acted as an attorney. The Williamsburg building served as his townhouse, as he  held residences in Yorktown and his manor-plantation house at Sandy Point, Charles City County. Presumably the house was built not long before 1740.

title: Locally made jamb brick for leaded casement window, 1667.
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Two room quarter but perhaps originally with only one door. Tenon and pinned rafters, originally with collars/ Used as a tenant house into the 20th century.

title: Parlor doorway, 1840s
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear.

Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. Second floor chamber over the hall, refitted in 1768 era.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Basement dining room mantel, 1840s
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

description: Townhouse of William Lightfoot.  William Byrd visited him here in June of 1740, both being on the Governor's Council at the time. Philip Lightfoot was one of the wealthiest planters of Virginia, owning plantations in York, Charles City, Surry, Goochland, Brunswick, New Kent and Hanover Counties. He was a merchant, acted as an attorney. The Williamsburg building served as his townhouse, as he  held residences in Yorktown and his manor-plantation house at Sandy Point, Charles City County. Presumably the house was built not long before 1740.

title: Kitchen, ca. 1750
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. title: Original room with kitchen beyond; 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. title: Mantel detail in inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Leaders of the newly formed Chesapeake Chapter of the VAF
headline: Beatty Cramer House, 9010 Liberty Road, Frederick, Maryland
description: H-bent, Dutch framing; dendro-dated 1746-48, with 1752-53 construction of partition to create twp rooms in main section. Originally 1 1/2 story with 2-story service (?) wing. Made into a convetional two-story house after 1855. Exposed framing, beaded corners outside, with crown molding molded into exterior face of the top plate. Exposed brick nogging in between laid in Flemish bond with roughly struck overhand joints. Series of porches built at the rear, the first of which included whitewash on the walls and later plaster. At some point porches are two story. Early porch off of service wing included floor levels at same same level as interior floors, which differ from the main section. Includes a later spring house and smokehouse. First fieldtrip of the newly innaugerated Tidewater Chapter of the Vernacular Architecture Forum.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Locally made mullion brick for leaded casement window, 1667
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Original chimney in hall; stairs were between fireplace and righ
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Neoclassical walnut mantel added in the early 19th century
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. Walnut (?) mantel in dining room, probably added first decade 19th century.
creator: Willie Graham

Philip Lightfoot was one of the wealthiest planters of Virginia, owning plantations in York, Charles City, Surry, Goochland, Brunswick, New Kent and Hanover Counties. He was a merchant, acted as an attorney. The Williamsburg building served as his townhouse, as he  held residences in Yorktown and his manor-plantation house at Sandy Point, Charles City County. title: Mantel in basement; ca. 1840
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Locally made mullion brick for leaded casement window, 1667
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Bill Lane, Jr, manager of the restoration. 2024
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90.

title: Terraced front yard, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Double-pen log quarter (saddleback); pit-sawn joists; wrought nails used in doors. Wooden hinges.

title: David Weir and John O'Rourke replacing portions of rotted sills
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting. Selective sill replacement on east wall.

Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.

title: Alleway in village
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Back stairs of original kitchen wing from quarters; 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Beatty Cramer House, 9010 Liberty Road, Frederick, Maryland
description: H-bent, Dutch framing; dendro-dated 1746-48, with 1752-53 construction of partition to create twp rooms in main section. Originally 1 1/2 story with 2-story service (?) wing. Made into a convetional two-story house after 1855. Exposed framing, beaded corners outside, with crown molding molded into exterior face of the top plate. Exposed brick nogging in between laid in Flemish bond with roughly struck overhand joints. Series of porches built at the rear, the first of which included whitewash on the walls and later plaster. At some point porches are two story. Early porch off of service wing included floor levels at same same level as interior floors, which differ from the main section. Includes a later spring house and smokehouse.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Latticed attic room; 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

The mantel in the chamber over the dining room was also replacced at that time and the closet doors added to its north side. The front and rear doors and frontispieces were replace/added in the 1830s or '40s.

creator: Willie Graham

title: Wooden fitting by hearth in double-pen log quarter; 2nd 1/4 19th
headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Double-pen log quarter (saddleback); pit-sawn joists; wrought nails used in doors. Wooden hinges.

title: 1840s replacement mantel
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: talheimer,hyde park,mantel,greek key,asher benjamin,plate 37,country builder's assisitant

title: South front laid in glazed-header Flemsih bond, ca. 1725. Arches
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Mnatel in 1st floor entertaining room, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Barn designed by Elmer J. Humphries, partner in Stafford & Humph
headline: Main Dairy Barn, Winterthur Garden and Museums, Winterthur, New Castle County, Delaware
description: Completed 1934. Shawver roof truss (introduced in 1904), includes laminated truss members set on 14' centers with common rafters in between. From Jonathan Poston: It was designed by Elmer Humphries who came in 1915 to design the complex.  He was a specialist in the making and use of concrete blocks. Below is info from Jeff Groff the Estate Historian and attached is an article and an ad for Humphries firm.  Interesting about the barn trusses.  Seems like Humphries was right on with the times on materials and structure. 'Elmer J. Humphries was a partner in Stafford & Humphries in NY state, a firm that built dairy facilities. I believe he was an engineer by training but need to look in my Humphries file in the Cottage. He was hired in 1915 to plan the dairy complex at Winterthur and he brought along the Hungarian workers who had built structures for him before.  In 1917 HF hired him to be Farm (Estate) Superintendent.  That was the year HF purchased what would become his Foundation Herd from a farm in Minnesota.  Humphries filled that role until his death in 1952 when he was succeeded by Leslie Potts (died 1966).'

description: Built 1705; expanded 1728, late 1750s; remodeled 1769; service wing built 1784. NW corner collapsed and was rebuilt in the 1840s. Cloverfields sold out of the Hemsley/Forman family to the Callahans in 1898; remodelled 1912. Image of the original 1705 shingle roof on the rear slope under the 1761 shed roof. Square butt Atlantic white cedar shingles, with approximately 7 exposure, nailed to riven oak clapboards. Clapboards were bent to form the valley with the original gable roof of the rear stair tower, most of which was removed when the shed roof was built. Valley had to shingles on it, although the shingles on the main roof swept towards it.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: White Swan Tavern, 231 High Street, Chestertown, Kent County, Maryland
description: Built as a 2 story dwelling about 17735, with a gambrel roof and a cellar. Internal chimneys were demolished and moved outboard around 1793; the roof rebuilt as a gable, and the street raised to hide the cellar windows. A brick shed was added to the rear. A frame structure possibly predating the house was appended to the rear. Excvavations on the site by the University of Delaware discovered numerous early ceramics including two large bowls with swan decoration, leading to naming of the place as the White Swan Tavern. However, no evidence of use as a tavern was dicovered in the documentary reccord.

title: West gable as reconstructed in 2009
headline: Charlton Coffeehouse, Duke of Gloucester Street, Williamsburg, Virginia
description: Built in 1750 as a storehouse and residence; repurposed as a coffeehouse in the 1760s and then as a dwelling at the end of the century. It was torn down in the late 19th century and replaced with a framed, Victorian house. Some of its foundations were preserved for reuse, and portions of its frame and trim were also reused  for framing and blocking in the new building. The Victorian house was moved off of its foundations by the Colonial Williamsburg Foundation and the Coffeehouse reconstructed, completed in 2009. Willie Graham and Mark Wenger undertook the invistigations to determine its original appearance and Willie took lead on the reconstruction design, drawings, and cconstruction oversight.

title: Strut to principal rafter joinery; 1804
headline: Rock Run, 4389 Rock Run Road, Havre de Grace, Harford County, Maryland
description: House built 1804 (date brick in gable). Two story, double pile with rear wing, the back of which was used as kitchen on the first floor, quarters on the second and attic floors, and for wet storage in the cellar. Note holes in sheathing possible for brackets on roof to hold slates while roofing (slater's bracket). This picture shows the strut which rises from the king post at the point where it joins the principal rafter. Note that the lower peg near this joint was intended to be pegged to hold the joint tight but the peg was never installed. Pegs along the top of the principal rafter are for an unknown purpose, perhaps suggesting the rafter was made from a reused timber. Note also the split-off end of the board made after the log from which it was fashioned was sawn at a sash mill. That board also has a lumber mark inscribed on its top face XXII.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: house,dwelling,truss,strut,principal rafter,slate,bracket,peg,unused peg hole,slate,bracket,slater's bracket,staging bracket,sash sawn,split end

title: Swirl marks on bottom of brick from moving off of molding table;
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

title: Town as viewed from ruins
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

A frame structure possibly predating the house was appended to the rear. Excvavations on the site by the University of Delaware discovered numerous early ceramics including two large bowls with swan decoration, leading to naming of the place as the White Swan Tavern. However, no evidence of use as a tavern was dicovered in the documentary reccord.

title: 1684 rafter reused in 1797 and scarfed to a lower member.
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. 1797 shed roof; upper portion of rafter reused from 1684 building, labled V (but perhaps with other portions of number missing).

title: Bill Lane, Jr, manager of the restoration. 2024
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90.

title: Mnatel in 1st floor entertaining room, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
