title: Quarter, 1840s
headline: Hyde Park Slave Quarter, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Burkeville, Nottoway County, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins of house
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: West gable on southwest corner, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham

Cloverfields sold out of the Hemsley/Forman family to the Callahans in 1898; remodelled 1912. Image of the original 1705 shingle roof on the rear slope under the 1761 shed roof. Square butt Atlantic white cedar shingles, with approximately 7 exposure, nailed to riven oak clapboards. Clapboards were bent to form the valley with the original gable roof of the rear stair tower, most of which was removed when the shed roof was built. Valley had to shingles on it, although the shingles on the main roof swept towards it.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Brick detail, kitchen, ca. 1750
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: 1-to-3 banding on gable with glazed header course banding; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

Two story timber frame double house with a rear and side piazza built at teh outset but remodeled with new porch posts and handrail around 1800. Drawing room (or hall) and withdrawing room on first floor street front. Ceneter stair passage opens to hall via an archway originally with no door. Two secondary rooms on rear flank teh stair passage. Cellars. Hall firebox originally had delft tile lining, but that was covered over around 1800 when the house was remodeled and the firebox given a new mantel. Front door converted to a window at this time and the piazza, as rebuilt, was given a door and frontispiece similar to what was being used at that time on single houses.Hall. Original archway with later gGreek revival doors and jambs inserted into the opening.

title: Archaeological ruins; site was used as headquarters for Manco In
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; site was used as headquarters for Manco In
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emporer Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Backstairs added 1784
headline: Toddsbury, 8777 Toddsbury Lane, Gloucester, Gloucester County, Virginia
description: Center passage plan brick house with rear ell, built with a gamrel roof. Probably constructed in the late 1750s, with an extension to the ell and a reconfiguration of this rear wing possibly in 1784. Two-story porch from the ouset; upper level enclosed as part of the 1783 remodeling. Main staircase is open string with diamond-set square balusters. Back stair, which was added in 1784, is closed string with turned balusters. Outbuildings currently under excavation date to the third quarter of the 18th century. Back stair added as part of the ca. 1784 alterations, when the passage was reated

title: Stair-head room, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Timber frame building pulled up to the rear of the house; 18th c
headline: White Swan Tavern, 231 High Street, Chestertown, Kent County, Maryland
description: Built as a 2 story dwelling about 17735, with a gambrel roof and a cellar. Internal chimneys were demolished and moved outboard around 1793; the roof rebuilt as a gable, and the street raised to hide the cellar windows. A brick shed was added to the rear. A frame structure possibly predating the house was appended to the rear. Excvavations on the site by the University of Delaware discovered numerous early ceramics including two large bowls with swan decoration, leading to naming of the place as the White Swan Tavern. However, no evidence of use as a tavern was dicovered in the documentary reccord.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Porch framing rebuilt about 1800 but pocketing replacement summe
headline: Philip Porcher House, 19 Archdale Street, Charleston, South Carolina UTM: 32.778807; -79.934791
description: Noted as newly built in a 1771 newspaper account. Two story timber frame double house with a rear and side piazza built at teh outset but remodeled with new porch posts and handrail around 1800. Drawing room (or hall) and withdrawing room on first floor street front. Ceneter stair passage opens to hall via an archway originally with no door. Two secondary rooms on rear flank teh stair passage. Cellars. Hall firebox originally had delft tile lining, but that was covered over around 1800 when the house was remodeled and the firebox given a new mantel. Front door converted to a window at this time and the piazza, as rebuilt, was given a door and frontispiece similar to what was being used at that time on single houses.Porch framing from cellar level. Mill sawn timbers without hewing. Framing was probably replaced about 1800 but with summers running into their original sockets in the masonry foundations.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Once blind dovetail hinge reset as surface mounted
headline: Hampton, 1950 Rutledge Road, McClellanville, Charleston County, South Carolina UTM: 33.201179; -79.434563
description: Built c. 1730 in frame with front section a full two stories and the back a single story; after the house sold in 1757 the house was raised to a full two stories across the back and a new principal rafter roof installed over it and two wings that were constructed to flank it, including a barrell-vaulted very large drawing room. Front porch said to date to 1790.Large drawing room with cove ceiling added to one end of the house when it was remodeled in the late 1750s.Presently referred to as the ballroom. Closet door with dovetail hinges meant to be hidden but now reset

title: Mission Concepcion
headline: Mission Concepcion, 807 Mission Road, San Antonio, Bexar County, Texas
description: Spanish Mission; National Historical Park and part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site. From Wikipedia Misión Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de Acuña was established in 1716 as Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de los Hainais in East Texas. The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. Located at 807 Mission Road, Mission Concepcion was designated a National Historic Landmark on April 15, 1970. Church dedicated in 1755.

title: Bowfat in parlor, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

title: Room in domestic dependency,  ca. 1760
headline: Lower Brandon, State Route 611, Burrowsville, Prince George County, Virginia, United States 37°15′27.1″N 76°59′36.2″W
description: Brandon, also known as Upper Brandon Plantation, was conceived as a five-part Palladian plan but built in stages, with the end pavillions first constructed sometime around 1760 and then the center block and hyphens after 1795. The two end wings were raised to two stories in the early 19th century, probably the 1810s. Owned by the Harrison from during the 1th century and until 1926. Restored by Robert Daniel in the 1920s. Domestic dependency; chimney front is probably earliest paneling in room; perhaps slightly later modern flush wainscit; later paneling over chair board but before window cut inflanking fireplace in 1810s.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Stair passage, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: South and west walls, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: church,brick,flemish bond,header,glaze,glazed header,glazed header flemish bond,patterned brickwork,anglican

title: Quarter, 1840s
headline: Hyde Park Slave Quarter, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Burkeville, Nottoway County, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: talheimer,hyde park,quarter,slave quarter,slave,enslaved,work,double quarter,slave house,timber frame,center chimney

title: Archaeological ruins of house
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Strut to principal rafter joinery; 1804
headline: Rock Run, 4389 Rock Run Road, Havre de Grace, Harford County, Maryland
description: House built 1804 (date brick in gable). Two story, double pile with rear wing, the back of which was used as kitchen on the first floor, quarters on the second and attic floors, and for wet storage in the cellar. Note holes in sheathing possible for brackets on roof to hold slates while roofing (slater's bracket). This picture shows the strut which rises from the king post at the point where it joins the principal rafter. Note that the lower peg near this joint was intended to be pegged to hold the joint tight but the peg was never installed. Pegs along the top of the principal rafter are for an unknown purpose, perhaps suggesting the rafter was made from a reused timber. Note also the split-off end of the board made after the log from which it was fashioned was sawn at a sash mill. That board also has a lumber mark inscribed on its top face XXII.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Riven clapboard sheathing bent at the valley of the sttair tower
headline: Cloverfields, 500 Forman Landing Road, Queenstown, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built 1705; expanded 1728, late 1750s; remodeled 1769; service wing built 1784. NW corner collapsed and was rebuilt in the 1840s. Cloverfields sold out of the Hemsley/Forman family to the Callahans in 1898; remodelled 1912. Image of the original 1705 shingle roof on the rear slope under the 1761 shed roof. Square butt Atlantic white cedar shingles, with approximately 7 exposure, nailed to riven oak clapboards. Clapboards were bent to form the valley with the original gable roof of the rear stair tower, most of which was removed when the shed roof was built. Valley had to shingles on it, although the shingles on the main roof swept towards it.

title: Glazed closer detail, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36.

title: Swirl marks on bottom of brick from moving off of molding table;
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.

title: Flemish bond front with glazed header banding/ 1-to-3 bond on ga
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Mantel in room next to office, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Toddsbury, 8777 Toddsbury Lane, Gloucester, Gloucester County, Virginia

headline: White Swan Tavern, 231 High Street, Chestertown, Kent County, Maryland
description: Built as a 2 story dwelling about 17735, with a gambrel roof and a cellar. Internal chimneys were demolished and moved outboard around 1793; the roof rebuilt as a gable, and the street raised to hide the cellar windows. A brick shed was added to the rear. A frame structure possibly predating the house was appended to the rear. Excvavations on the site by the University of Delaware discovered numerous early ceramics including two large bowls with swan decoration, leading to naming of the place as the White Swan Tavern. However, no evidence of use as a tavern was dicovered in the documentary reccord.

title: Peg holes driven into window jamb when casement converted to sas
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90.

title: Front elevation, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: house,dwelling,brick,masonry,glazed header flemish bond,flemish bond,dormer,balcony,window,cellar window

headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: rear roof slope with riven wind beam
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Ruled joints, front elevation, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Stair detail, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Slop-molded (water-struck) bricks, west gable, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: church,brick,flemish bond,header,glaze,glazed header,glazed header flemish bond,patterned brickwork,anglican

title: Strut to principal rafter joinery; 1804
headline: Rock Run, 4389 Rock Run Road, Havre de Grace, Harford County, Maryland
description: House built 1804 (date brick in gable). Two story, double pile with rear wing, the back of which was used as kitchen on the first floor, quarters on the second and attic floors, and for wet storage in the cellar. Note holes in sheathing possible for brackets on roof to hold slates while roofing (slater's bracket). This picture shows the strut which rises from the king post at the point where it joins the principal rafter. Note that the lower peg near this joint was intended to be pegged to hold the joint tight but the peg was never installed. Pegs along the top of the principal rafter are for an unknown purpose, perhaps suggesting the rafter was made from a reused timber. Note also the split-off end of the board made after the log from which it was fashioned was sawn at a sash mill. That board also has a lumber mark inscribed on its top face XXII.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: house,dwelling,truss,strut,principal rafter,slate,bracket,peg,unused peg hole,slate,bracket,slater's bracket,staging bracket,sash sawn,split end

title: Riven clapboard sheathing bent at the valley of the sttair tower
headline: Cloverfields, 500 Forman Landing Road, Queenstown, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built 1705; expanded 1728, late 1750s; remodeled 1769; service wing built 1784. NW corner collapsed and was rebuilt in the 1840s. Cloverfields sold out of the Hemsley/Forman family to the Callahans in 1898; remodelled 1912. Image of the original 1705 shingle roof on the rear slope under the 1761 shed roof. Square butt Atlantic white cedar shingles, with approximately 7 exposure, nailed to riven oak clapboards. Clapboards were bent to form the valley with the original gable roof of the rear stair tower, most of which was removed when the shed roof was built. Valley had to shingles on it, although the shingles on the main roof swept towards it.

Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36.

headline: Mission Concepcion, 807 Mission Road, San Antonio, Bexar County, Texas
description: Spanish Mission; National Historical Park and part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site. From Wikipedia Misión Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de Acuña was established in 1716 as Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de los Hainais in East Texas. The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. Located at 807 Mission Road, Mission Concepcion was designated a National Historic Landmark on April 15, 1970. Church dedicated in 1755.

title: Casement window. Coping of jamb, with cove, to hgead, with ogee;
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90.

title: Stairs to attic, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Brick locally made in bottomless mold; 1667
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Window detail in the parlor 1750s
headline: Toddsbury, 8777 Toddsbury Lane, Gloucester, Gloucester County, Virginia
description: Center passage plan brick house with rear ell, built with a gamrel roof. Probably constructed in the late 1750s, with an extension to the ell and a reconfiguration of this rear wing possibly in 1784. Two-story porch from the ouset; upper level enclosed as part of the 1783 remodeling. Main staircase is open string with diamond-set square balusters. Back stair, which was added in 1784, is closed string with turned balusters. Outbuildings currently under excavation date to the third quarter of the 18th century. Parlor (west room). Window treatment

title: Kitchen, ca. 1750
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Gable end showing raised front wall (1768)
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Riven clapboard sheathing bent at the valley of the sttair tower
headline: Cloverfields, 500 Forman Landing Road, Queenstown, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built 1705; expanded 1728, late 1750s; remodeled 1769; service wing built 1784. NW corner collapsed and was rebuilt in the 1840s. Cloverfields sold out of the Hemsley/Forman family to the Callahans in 1898; remodelled 1912. Image of the original 1705 shingle roof on the rear slope under the 1761 shed roof. Square butt Atlantic white cedar shingles, with approximately 7 exposure, nailed to riven oak clapboards. Clapboards were bent to form the valley with the original gable roof of the rear stair tower, most of which was removed when the shed roof was built. Valley had to shingles on it, although the shingles on the main roof swept towards it.

title: Glazed headers on corner, west gable, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: church,brick,flemish bond,header,glaze,glazed header,glazed header flemish bond,patterned brickwork,anglican

title: Quarter, 1840s
headline: Hyde Park Slave Quarter, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Burkeville, Nottoway County, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: 1-to-3 banding on gable with glazed header course banding; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: house,dwelling,1 to 3 bond,3 to 1 bond,1 to 3,3 to 1,glaze,glazing,band,banding,glazed banding,chimney

title: View from across cemetery, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham

title: VMFA tour group (2024) at the unfinished wall of Six Monoliths a
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: vmfa,inca,ollantaytambo,sacred valley,manco inca,native american,spamish,conquistador,stone,masonry,fort,fortification,headquarter,pachacuti,temple,sun temple,unfinished wall,six monoliths

title: Mantel detail in inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Main Dairy Barn #40, Winterthur Garden and Museums, Winterthur, New Castle County, Delaware
description: Completed 1934. Shawver roof truss (introduced in 1904), includes laminated truss members set on 14' centers with common rafters in between. From Jonathan Poston: It was designed by Elmer Humphries who came in 1915 to design the complex.  He was a specialist in the making and use of concrete blocks. Below is info from Jeff Groff the Estate Historian and attached is an article and an ad for Humphries firm.  Interesting about the barn trusses.  Seems like Humphries was right on with the times on materials and structure. 'Elmer J. Humphries was a partner in Stafford & Humphries in NY state, a firm that built dairy facilities. I believe he was an engineer by training but need to look in my Humphries file in the Cottage. He was hired in 1915 to plan the dairy complex at Winterthur and he brought along the Hungarian workers who had built structures for him before.  In 1917 HF hired him to be Farm (Estate) Superintendent.  That was the year HF purchased what would become his Foundation Herd from a farm in Minnesota.  Humphries filled that role until his death in 1952 when he was succeeded by Leslie Potts (died 1966).'
creator: Willie Graham
categories: barn,dairy,dairy barn,henry francis dupont,dupont,winterthur,shawver truss,gambrel,gambrel roof,dutch roof,elmer humphries,engineer

title: Slate samples nailed to rafters during each re-roofing
headline: Judge Robert Pringle House, 70 Tradd Street, Charleston, South Carolina
description: Three story, brick single house. Built by Pringle in 1774. Slate probably first added to the house in the late 18th century. If originally covered with wooden shingles, they probably were laid on skip lath. (Moby Marks observes that is the typical treatment in Charleston.) The current covering is solid sheathing, but joints, made of sash-sawn pine and ws secured with hand-forged wrought-iron nails. Slaters in the 19th century (as well as post-Hugo work) nailed samples of their slate to the sides of rafters.

title: Locally made brick, 1667. Struck face defaced by raim before fir
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.

title: Mantel detail in inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

description: Built as a 2 story dwelling about 17735, with a gambrel roof and a cellar. Internal chimneys were demolished and moved outboard around 1793; the roof rebuilt as a gable, and the street raised to hide the cellar windows. A brick shed was added to the rear. A frame structure possibly predating the house was appended to the rear. Excvavations on the site by the University of Delaware discovered numerous early ceramics including two large bowls with swan decoration, leading to naming of the place as the White Swan Tavern. However, no evidence of use as a tavern was dicovered in the documentary reccord.

title: Chimney flues meeting to a single stack, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Stair detail, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins and village; granary on mountain in backgro
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Wooden pintle from hinge on latticed attic room; 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: 1-to-3 banding on gable with glazed header course banding; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Glazed headers on corner of south wall at east end, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.

title: Slop-molded (or water-struck) bricks, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham

title: 1760s two-story house waised slightly and cross gable added in 1
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Mantel in inner room, 1720s
