description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

description: Spanish Mission; National Historical Park and part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site. From Wikipedia Misión Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de Acuña was established in 1716 as Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de los Hainais in East Texas. The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. title: Attic window; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: HOuse built about 1771; porch rebuilt 1800
headline: Philip Porcher House, 19 Archdale Street, Charleston, South Carolina UTM: 32.778807; -79.934791
description: Noted as newly built in a 1771 newspaper account. Two story timber frame double house with a rear and side piazza built at teh outset but remodeled with new porch posts and handrail around 1800. Drawing room (or hall) and withdrawing room on first floor street front. Ceneter stair passage opens to hall via an archway originally with no door. Two secondary rooms on rear flank teh stair passage. Cellars. Hall firebox originally had delft tile lining, but that was covered over around 1800 when the house was remodeled and the firebox given a new mantel. Front door converted to a window at this time and the piazza, as rebuilt, was given a door and frontispiece similar to what was being used at that time on single houses.Garden facade of house, including original porch that was rebuilt at least above the piers and below the roof about 1800
creator: Willie Graham

Made into a convetional two-story house after 1855. Exposed framing, beaded corners outside, with crown molding molded into exterior face of the top plate. Exposed brick nogging in between laid in Flemish bond with roughly struck overhand joints. Series of porches built at the rear, the first of which included whitewash on the walls and later plaster. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

title: 1797 wall framing, east wall, with many reused timbers
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting.

title: Built late 18th c; modiied in the 1830s; attic access
headline: Sotterley Corn Crib, 44300 Sotterley Lane, Hollywood, Maryland UTM: 38.376470, -76.542078
description: Timber framed corn crib probably built in the late 18th century fashioned out of an earlier building parts and then remodeled in the 1830s or 40s. Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Back gable wall sheathed and covered with thin, wooden shingles, recently replaced with new shingles. Tilited false plate; post bays; sidewall assembly. Hewn oak rafters set flat. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins and village; granary on mountain in backgro
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Mission Concepcion
headline: Mission Concepcion, 807 Mission Road, San Antonio, Bexar County, Texas
description: Spanish Mission; National Historical Park and part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site. From Wikipedia Misión Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de Acuña was established in 1716 as Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de los Hainais in East Texas. The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. Located at 807 Mission Road, Mission Concepcion was designated a National Historic Landmark on April 15, 1970. Church dedicated in 1755.

title: West door with rebuilt arch; ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: church,brick,flemish bond,header,glaze,glazed header,glazed header flemish bond,patterned brickwork,anglican

title: Water-struck brick made in bottomless mold but set on sand bed;
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: 1-to-3 banding on gable with glazed header course banding; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland

title: Glazed headers used on corner, south facade, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: church,brick,flemish bond,header,glaze,glazed header,glazed header flemish bond,patterned brickwork,anglican

title: Mantel detail in inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. title: Reused 1684 stud, with carpenter mark X from original use  (17
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting.

The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. Located at 807 Mission Road, Mission Concepcion was designated a National Historic Landmark on April 15, 1970. Church dedicated in 1755.

title: Bowfat in parlor, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Locally made mullion and window jamb bricks for leaded casement
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

Two-story porch from the ouset; upper level enclosed as part of the 1783 remodeling. Main staircase is open string with diamond-set square balusters. Back stair, which was added in 1784, is closed string with turned balusters. Outbuildings currently under excavation date to the third quarter of the 18th century. title: Stairs to attic at queen post truss; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Mantel replaced in the hall about 1800
headline: Philip Porcher House, 19 Archdale Street, Charleston, South Carolina UTM: 32.778807; -79.934791
description: Noted as newly built in a 1771 newspaper account. Two story timber frame double house with a rear and side piazza built at the outset but remodeled with new porch posts and handrail around 1800. Drawing room (or hall) and withdrawing room on first floor street front. Ceneter stair passage opens to hall via an archway originally with no door. Two secondary rooms on rear flank teh stair passage. Cellars. Hall firebox originally had delft tile lining, but that was covered over around 1800 when the house was remodeled and the firebox given a new mantel. Front door converted to a window at this time and the piazza, as rebuilt, was given a door and frontispiece similar to what was being used at that time on single houses.Mantel in hall was replaced about 1800. Fireplace infilled at that time. Tommy Bennett removed the infill during his ownership (in the 1980s?) and sold off the mantel; the current owner, Price Cameron, bought back the mantel and reinstalled it. Hidden behind the federal fireplace in fill was an original installation of Delft tiles.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; site was used as headquarters for Manco In
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Locally made mullion and window jamb bricks for leaded casement
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Built late 18th c; modiied in the 1830s; windless
headline: Sotterley Corn Crib, 44300 Sotterley Lane, Hollywood, Maryland UTM: 38.376470, -76.542078
description: Timber framed corn crib probably built in the late 18th century fashioned out of an earlier building parts and then remodeled in the 1830s or 40s. Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Back gable wall sheathed and covered with thin, wooden shingles, recently replaced with new shingles. Tilited false plate; post bays; sidewall assembly. Hewn oak rafters set flat. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. title: Mantel in inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

title: Reused 1684 stud, with carpenter mark partially severed (1797 re
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting. Reused 1684 stud, with carpenter mark partially severed (1797 reuse).

title: Barn designed by Elmer J. Humphries, partner in Stafford & Humph
headline: Main Dairy Barn #40, Winterthur Garden and Museums, Winterthur, New Castle County, Delaware
description: Completed 1934. Shawver roof truss (introduced in 1904), includes laminated truss members set on 14' centers with common rafters in between. From Jonathan Poston: It was designed by Elmer Humphries who came in 1915 to design the complex.  He was a specialist in the making and use of concrete blocks. Below is info from Jeff Groff the Estate Historian and attached is an article and an ad for Humphries firm.  Interesting about the barn trusses.  Seems like Humphries was right on with the times on materials and structure. 'Elmer J. Humphries was a partner in Stafford & Humphries in NY state, a firm that built dairy facilities. I believe he was an engineer by training but need to look in my Humphries file in the Cottage. He was hired in 1915 to plan the dairy complex at Winterthur and he brought along the Hungarian workers who had built structures for him before.  In 1917 HF hired him to be Farm (Estate) Superintendent.  That was the year HF purchased what would become his Foundation Herd from a farm in Minnesota.  Humphries filled that role until his death in 1952 when he was succeeded by Leslie Potts (died 1966).'

title: Town, with Inca ruins in background
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: 1-to-3 banding on gable with glazed header course banding; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Glazed header Flemsih bond; apse (ca. 1725)
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: house,dwelling,flemish bond,glazed header flemish bond,glaze,glazing,stringcourse,water table

title: East gable and apse, ca. 1725
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: church,brick,flemish bond,header,glaze,glazed header,glazed header flemish bond,patterned brickwork,anglican

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins of house
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Valley rafter detail for now missing rear wing; 1714
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear.

headline: Mission Concepcion, 807 Mission Road, San Antonio, Bexar County, Texas
description: Spanish Mission; National Historical Park and part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site. From Wikipedia Misión Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de Acuña was established in 1716 as Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de los Hainais in East Texas. The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. Located at 807 Mission Road, Mission Concepcion was designated a National Historic Landmark on April 15, 1970. Church dedicated in 1755.

title: Built late 18th c; modiied in the 1830s; windless
headline: Sotterley Corn Crib, 44300 Sotterley Lane, Hollywood, Maryland UTM: 38.376470, -76.542078
description: Timber framed corn crib probably built in the late 18th century fashioned out of an earlier building parts and then remodeled in the 1830s or 40s. Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Back gable wall sheathed and covered with thin, wooden shingles, recently replaced with new shingles. Tilited false plate; post bays; sidewall assembly. Hewn oak rafters set flat. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Brace labled I. Possible reused down brace from 1784 building
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting. Possible reused down brace from 1784 building on 1797 shed wall. Labeled I.

title: Queen post truss, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Lower level of porch; rebuilt about 1800
headline: Philip Porcher House, 19 Archdale Street, Charleston, South Carolina UTM: 32.778807; -79.934791
description: Noted as newly built in a 1771 newspaper account. Two story timber frame double house with a rear and side piazza built at teh outset but remodeled with new porch posts and handrail around 1800. Drawing room (or hall) and withdrawing room on first floor street front. Ceneter stair passage opens to hall via an archway originally with no door. Two secondary rooms on rear flank teh stair passage. Cellars. Hall firebox originally had delft tile lining, but that was covered over around 1800 when the house was remodeled and the firebox given a new mantel. Front door converted to a window at this time and the piazza, as rebuilt, was given a door and frontispiece similar to what was being used at that time on single houses.Porch framing from cellar level. Mill sawn timbers without hewing. Framing was probably replaced about 1800 but with summers running into their original sockets in the masonry foundations.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Rear stretcher face of jamb bricks for leaded casement window, 1
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Paving under porch as redone about 1800
headline: Philip Porcher House, 19 Archdale Street, Charleston, South Carolina UTM: 32.778807; -79.934791
description: Noted as newly built in a 1771 newspaper account. Two story timber frame double house with a rear and side piazza built at teh outset but remodeled with new porch posts and handrail around 1800. Drawing room (or hall) and withdrawing room on first floor street front. Ceneter stair passage opens to hall via an archway originally with no door. Two secondary rooms on rear flank teh stair passage. Cellars. Hall firebox originally had delft tile lining, but that was covered over around 1800 when the house was remodeled and the firebox given a new mantel. Front door converted to a window at this time and the piazza, as rebuilt, was given a door and frontispiece similar to what was being used at that time on single houses.Early brick paving under porch

title: Ladder stair probably used for roof access, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Swirl marks on brick made sliding green brick on table when form
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.

A frame structure possibly predating the house was appended to the rear. Excvavations on the site by the University of Delaware discovered numerous early ceramics including two large bowls with swan decoration, leading to naming of the place as the White Swan Tavern. title: Stairs 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

title: Partial sill replaccement on east wall (1797 section).
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting. Selective sill replacement on east wall.

title: Archaeological ruins of house
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

Plaster walls, chair board, exposed ceiling with hand-planed floorboards above, which were secured in place with pegs instead of nails. Unheated upper floor. Lower story includes trough for water and smoke hood presumably for drying vegetation, although seemingly little used. Details of cellar window. Note that the shutter is made with a dovetailed batten held in place with two pegs.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Flemish bond front with glazed header banding/ 1-to-3 bond on ga
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Finer glazed-header Flemsih on south side of apse than north, ca
headline: Trinity Anglican Church, “Old Dorchester Church,” 1716 Taylors Island Road, Woolford; Dorchester County, Maryland
description: Though claimed to date to the 17th century, the church was likely dated about 1720 (Carl Lounsbury suggests it was built early 18th century). Most of its original finishes and fittings are now replaced, but its form and brickwork are suggestive of a first quarter 18th century date and likely at the end of that date range. Flemish bond brickwork shows up in the region at the end of the 17th century, which was used here. However, the general pattern on Maryland buildings well into the 18th century is to use English bond on lesser walls and in the plinth. Here, Flemish was used for all--plinth and all four walls, including the apse. There was an attempt to use glazed headers on all walls, but clearly not enough were available and darker bricks, poorly glazed headers were used on lesser walls until even those were lacking. The wide windows suggest they were built as casements, which by the 1730s seems like a less likely choice for the openings. The church was restored between 1953-60.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: church,brick,flemish bond,header,glaze,glazed header,glazed header flemish bond,patterned brickwork,anglican

title: Alleway in village
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Floorboards handplaned underneath and marked
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Kitchen hearth and Scotch ovenl 1765; heavily restored 1950s
headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Built late 18th c; modiied in the 1830s. Wagon access to loft
headline: Sotterley Corn Crib, 44300 Sotterley Lane, Hollywood, Maryland UTM: 38.376470, -76.542078
description: Timber framed corn crib probably built in the late 18th century fashioned out of an earlier building parts and then remodeled in the 1830s or 40s. Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Back gable wall sheathed and covered with thin, wooden shingles, recently replaced with new shingles. Tilited false plate; post bays; sidewall assembly. Hewn oak rafters set flat. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.

title: Bill lane, Jr., west side pulling replacement sill on east into
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting.

title: Servant spaces over kitchen extension of ca. 1800; walls raised
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Street faacde; 1735
headline: John Buck House, 103 North Queen Street, Chestertown, Kent County, Maryland
description: Patrick Henry Residence, Washington College. Also known as the Buck-Chambers House. Constructed on Lot 34, Chestertown. From MHT MEDUSA: Lot No. 34 in Chestertown contains two of the earliest surviving houses in Chestertown, both built in the mid-1730's for an absentee owner. When Jonathan Page, mariner, sold the northwest half of lot 34 in 1735, the deed stated that it  is now marked out for building . . . opposite a dwelling house lately built by one James Moore (bricklayer)”  (Moore's house was probably located on the north comer of High and Queen Streets). John Buck, Esquire, of Bideford, Devonshire, Great Brittain, soon unproved his lot with the construction of not one, but two houses, built of a combination of brick and stone.

title: Leaders of the newly formed Chesapeake Chapter of the VAF
headline: Beatty Cramer House, 9010 Liberty Road, Frederick, Maryland
description: H-bent, Dutch framing; dendro-dated 1746-48, with 1752-53 construction of partition to create twp rooms in main section. Originally 1 1/2 story with 2-story service (?) wing. Made into a convetional two-story house after 1855. Exposed framing, beaded corners outside, with crown molding molded into exterior face of the top plate. Exposed brick nogging in between laid in Flemish bond with roughly struck overhand joints. Series of porches built at the rear, the first of which included whitewash on the walls and later plaster. At some point porches are two story. Early porch off of service wing included floor levels at same same level as interior floors, which differ from the main section. Includes a later spring house and smokehouse. First fieldtrip of the newly innaugerated Tidewater Chapter of the Vernacular Architecture Forum. Our fearless leaders--Lisa Davidson, Marcia Miller, Jeff Klee, Catherine Lavoie, and Heather Barrett
creator: Willie Graham
categories: vernacular architecture forum,vaf,chesapeake chapter,lisa davidson,marcia miller,jeff klee,catherine lavoie,heather barrett

title: Ruled joints and red wash detail, ca. 1740
headline: Colonel Philip Lightfoot House, 302 Francis Street East, Williamsburg, Virginia
description: Townhouse of William Lightfoot.  William Byrd visited him here in June of 1740, both being on the Governor's Council at the time. Philip Lightfoot was one of the wealthiest planters of Virginia, owning plantations in York, Charles City, Surry, Goochland, Brunswick, New Kent and Hanover Counties. He was a merchant, acted as an attorney. The Williamsburg building served as his townhouse, as he  held residences in Yorktown and his manor-plantation house at Sandy Point, Charles City County. Presumably the house was built not long before 1740.

headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Weiss Erkelens and Cary Carson examining excavations of outbuild
headline: Toddsbury, 8777 Toddsbury Lane, Gloucester, Gloucester County, Virginia
description: Center passage plan brick house with rear ell, built with a gamrel roof. Probably constructed in the late 1750s, with an extension to the ell and a reconfiguration of this rear wing possibly in 1784. Two-story porch from the ouset; upper level enclosed as part of the 1783 remodeling. Main staircase is open string with diamond-set square balusters. Back stair, which was added in 1784, is closed string with turned balusters. Outbuildings currently under excavation date to the third quarter of the 18th century. Outbuilings dating to the 3rd quarter of the 18th century under excavation. North wall of outbuildings align with the south wall of the main house. Cary Carson and Weis examining archaeology with current owner.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Detail of Monk bond on front of original section, 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Detail of Inca stonewrok
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: 1871 plat
headline: Lower Brandon, State Route 611, Burrowsville, Prince George County, Virginia, United States 37°15′27.1″N 76°59′36.2″W
description: 1871 plat of Brandon for George Harrison by E. A Pillow, Surveyor. Brandon, also known as Upper Brandon Plantation, was conceived as a five-part Palladian plan but built in stages, with the end pavillions first constructed sometime around 1760 and then the center block and hyphens after 1795. The two end wings were raised to two stories in teh early 19th century, probably the 1810s. Owned by the Harrison from during the 1th century and until 1926. Restored by Robert Daniel in the 1920s.

Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.

headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Double-pen log quarter (saddleback); pit-sawn joists; wrought nails used in doors. Wooden hinges.

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Stairs to attic at queen post truss; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham
