title: Hall window with vallaence that probably slightly post dates the
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. Hall window with vallaence that probably slightly post dates the 1768 remodeling of the room.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Attic of quarter
headline: Hyde Park Slave Quarter, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Burkeville, Nottoway County, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Gauged and underccut flooring; 18th century
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Clapboards used to sweep the valley where a rear wing once met t
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. Clapboards bent along angled member to create a swept valley where the rear wing intersected the front roof.

title: Kitchen hearth and Scotch ovenl 1765; heavily restored 1950s
headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. title: Stairs to attic, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; site was used as headquarters for Manco In
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: David Weir replacing portions of rotted sills on the east (1797)
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting. Selective sill replacement on east wall.

headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.

title: Second-story porch along rear of service wing; 2nd half 19th cen
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Privy, 2nd quarter 19th century
headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36.

title: Rear wing likely extended in 1784
headline: Toddsbury, 8777 Toddsbury Lane, Gloucester, Gloucester County, Virginia
description: Center passage plan brick house with rear ell, built with a gamrel roof. Probably constructed in the late 1750s, with an extension to the ell and a reconfiguration of this rear wing possibly in 1784. Two-story porch from the ouset; upper level enclosed as part of the 1783 remodeling. Main staircase is open string with diamond-set square balusters. Back stair, which was added in 1784, is closed string with turned balusters. Outbuildings currently under excavation date to the third quarter of the 18th century.

title: 1852-53 partition
headline: Beatty Cramer House, 9010 Liberty Road, Frederick, Maryland
description: H-bent, Dutch framing; dendro-dated 1746-48, with 1752-53 construction of partition to create twp rooms in main section. Originally 1 1/2 story with 2-story service (?) wing. Made into a convetional two-story house after 1855. Exposed framing, beaded corners outside, with crown molding molded into exterior face of the top plate. Exposed brick nogging in between laid in Flemish bond with roughly struck overhand joints. Series of porches built at the rear, the first of which included whitewash on the walls and later plaster. At some point porches are two story. Early porch off of service wing included floor levels at same same level as interior floors, which differ from the main section. Includes a later spring house and smokehouse.
creator: Willie Graham

description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Latticed attic room; 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Ekeko, God of abundance, fertility, and joy
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; site was used as headquarters for Manco In
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.

title: Kitchen hearth and Scotch ovenl 1765; heavily restored 1950s
headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. title: Double quarter, ca. 1840
headline: Hyde Park Slave Quarter, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Burkeville, Nottoway County, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: talheimer,hyde park,quarter,slave quarter,slave,enslaved,work,double quarter,slave house,timber frame,center chimney

title: Mantel in inner room, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Mission Concepcion, 807 Mission Road, San Antonio, Bexar County, Texas
description: Spanish Mission; National Historical Park and part of a UNESCO World Heritage Site. From Wikipedia Misión Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de Acuña was established in 1716 as Nuestra Señora de la Purísima Concepción de los Hainais in East Texas. The mission was moved in 1731 to San Antonio. Founded by Franciscan friars, this is the best preserved of the Texas missions. Located at 807 Mission Road, Mission Concepcion was designated a National Historic Landmark on April 15, 1970. Church dedicated in 1755.

title: Stairs at landing, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Dining room mantel with added cornice
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. Dining room manetl, presumably early 19th century, laid over 1768 panel that was fitted above the mantel from that era. Rest of paneling removed from this wall. Mantel cornice dates to the second quarter of the 19th century. Hard to figure out this dining room mantel. The raised panel is all that remains of the 1767/8 paneled end wall. The bolection mold, which is awfully light and fussy, probably dates to the early 19th century. The radiator-fin-looking cornice clearly dates to the second quarter of the 19th century. Not sure what to make of the marble. Also has a rather nice Victorian coal grate front.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Window arch with ruled joints, 1768
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear.

description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Double-pen log quarter (saddleback); pit-sawn joists; wrought nails used in doors. Wooden hinges.

title: Brick detail, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.

title: Detail of Monk bond on front of original section, 1760s
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. title: John O'Rourke easing replaccement sill in place
headline: Third Haven Meeting House, 405 South Washington Street, Talbot County, Maryland
description: Built 1684 as a T-plan, timber frame Quaker meeting house with sills, which sat on the ground. Raised onto cedar blocks in 1698. About 1709 its floor framing was repalcced and some of the posts. At the same time Elder-benches were installed at the 2 ends of the main room. About 1750 the benches were moved to their present location on the east wall. In 1796-97, the leg of the T--which housed teh original entry--was removed and a shed addition was built in its placce on the front to provide for a larger meeting area. A partition was built in the center with sliding dividers and the Elders' bench was extended at both ends. The current stairs were built at this time as well. Around 1870 the front was moved to the south gable. Around 1930 the building was restored in anticipation of the 1934 anniversary of its construction. The building was again restored in 1989-90. Current project includes repair to framing and replacement of decayed modern siding and trim in preperation for painting. Selective sill replacement on east wall.

The two end wings were raised to two stories in teh early 19th century, probably the 1810s. Owned by the Harrison from during the 1th century and until 1926. Restored by Robert Daniel in the 1920s.Stair refashioned and arches added about 1830

title: Built late 18th c; modiied in the 1830s; board and batten door w
headline: Sotterley Corn Crib, 44300 Sotterley Lane, Hollywood, Maryland UTM: 38.376470, -76.542078
description: Timber framed corn crib probably built in the late 18th century fashioned out of an earlier building parts and then remodeled in the 1830s or 40s. Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. Back gable wall sheathed and covered with thin, wooden shingles, recently replaced with new shingles. Tilited false plate; post bays; sidewall assembly. Hewn oak rafters set flat. Attic has sheathed kneewalls and bins around the sides created in the 1830s, with no flooring in the center third running front to back. Doorway to attic on side bear the front through the roof. Overhang on front gable created in 1830s by puching the lower part of the gable back 4'.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Detail of Inca stonewrok
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Scotch oven in kitchen; 1765
headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. Pocket in masonry below string course for early porch

title: Cast-iron butt hinge oddly mounted on parlor doorway, 1840s
headline: Hyde Park, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Nottoway County, Burkeville, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. Hyde Park was improved with significant additions and alterations, reportedly in 1906. The interior work attempted to blend with the c. 1840s changes to the house. Improvements included rebuilding of the staircase to make it a gentler run. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.

title: View of roof slope over stair
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear.

title: Kitchen; 1765; dresser likley 1950s built of 18th-century parts
headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. title: Locally made mullion brick for leaded casement window, 1667
headline: Chapel, St. Mary's City, St. Mary's County, Maryland
description: Built 1667. Excavated by St. Mary's City. Locally-made bricks, including wall, mullion and jamb bricks. Water struck in a bottomless mold with irregular surface of sand on mollding table. Dried by setting base of green bricks on grass or straw and set flat. Clay appears to have been excessively wet. Some bricks, especially the less fired ones, have few inclusions; many have iron inclusions, some excessive amounts, which left iron spots on the bricks. The iron may have been from naturally occuring iron fragments in the local clay. Brick Catholic Church with leaded casement window frames set in brick opeings with cast-brick mullion and jamb bricks.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Home house quarter, ca. 1850
headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Two room quarter but perhaps originally with only one door. Tenon and pinned rafters, originally with collars/ Used as a tenant house into the 20th century.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Archaeological ruins; detail of Inca stonework
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Beatty Cramer House, 9010 Liberty Road, Frederick, Maryland
description: H-bent, Dutch framing; dendro-dated 1746-48, with 1752-53 construction of partition to create twp rooms in main section. Originally 1 1/2 story with 2-story service (?) wing. Made into a convetional two-story house after 1855. Exposed framing, beaded corners outside, with crown molding molded into exterior face of the top plate. Exposed brick nogging in between laid in Flemish bond with roughly struck overhand joints. Series of porches built at the rear, the first of which included whitewash on the walls and later plaster. At some point porches are two story. Early porch off of service wing included floor levels at same same level as interior floors, which differ from the main section. Includes a later spring house and smokehouse.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Brick detail, 1720s
headline: Bellaire Manor, 1900 Pattinson Avenue, Farimont Park, Philadelphia, Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania
description: Thought to have been built sometime between 1714 and 1735. 1720s seems a likely date. Originally known as Bell Air; also Singley House, Lasse Cock's Manor House, Samuel Preston House.. Built by Samuel Preston (1665-1743); alderman, Quaker, William Penn's executor; Philadelphia mayor 1711-12; Provincial Treasurer 1714-1743.. Samuel Preston and Henry Lawrence were executors of John Vaughan's estate, who owned the property. They sold the property in 1713 to Richard Moore, Preston's son-in-law for L140. In 1714. Preston bought it back from Moore for the same amount. In 1735, Preston had the land resurveyed and a grant from Wm Penn for the same. The patent gave Preston 142 acres including the houses, buildings, improvements . . .etc. % months later the Preston couple sold the land to Alexander Woodrop for L1000.  (twice the acreage of what he purchased in 1714 but about 8 times what he paid for it). When Huey and Elizabeth Davey's,, later owners, died (she in 1782), her will gave to her son Alexander the house, kitchen with the other buildings &c.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: house,dwelling,brick,mortar,joint,morttar joint,ruled joint,glaze,glazing,glazed header flemish bons,flemish bond

title: Banbury lock on stair door, double-pen log quarter. 2nd 1/4 19th
headline: Pharsalia, 2333 Pharsalia Road, Massies Mill, Nelson County, Virginia
description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Double-pen log quarter (saddleback); pit-sawn joists; wrought nails used in doors. Wooden hinges.

title: Original second floor quarter above kitchen; 1760s. Walls raised
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

headline: Colonel Philip Lightfoot House, 302 Francis Street East, Williamsburg, Virginia
description: Townhouse of William Lightfoot.  William Byrd visited him here in June of 1740, both being on the Governor's Council at the time. Philip Lightfoot was one of the wealthiest planters of Virginia, owning plantations in York, Charles City, Surry, Goochland, Brunswick, New Kent and Hanover Counties. He was a merchant, acted as an attorney. The Williamsburg building served as his townhouse, as he  held residences in Yorktown and his manor-plantation house at Sandy Point, Charles City County. Presumably the house was built not long before 1740.

Presumably the house was built not long before 1740.

title: Archaeological ruins; site was used as headquarters for Manco In
headline: Ollantaytambo, Sacred Valley, Urubamba Province, Peru
description: A termple and fortress where Incan emperor Pachacuti built his estate after conquering it in the mid 1400s. From website https://www.peruforless.com/blog/ollantaytambo: Ollantaytambo was one of many settlements conquered by Pachacutec, the 9th Inca king who commandeered the transformation of the Cusco Kingdom into the powerful Tawantinsuyu or Inca Empire. Under his reign, Ollantaytambo served as a pivotal outpost for Inca nobles and agrarian laborers. Upon his death, the estate remained under the control of his lineage in accordance with the land privileges reserved for Inca kings. When the rebel leader Manco Inca escaped from Cusco, he established his headquarters here and it became the site of the infamous Battle of Ollantaytambo in 1537. Facing an incoming attack, Manco Inca ordered the building of a river dam to flood the valley and used the terraces as higher ground to defeat the Spanish soldiers. The Inca rebel forces eventually retreated further into the jungle, and Ollantaytambo fell firmly into the possession of the Spanish colonists. The town became a focal point for settlement in the Sacred Valley – first as anencomienda granted to Hernando Pizarro, brother of Conqueror Francisco Pizarro; and then as an important source of tribute and labor for the colonial state. (Encomiendas were parcels of land given to Spanish settlers. In feudal-like fashion, the grantee was entitled to the labor of native communities who resided therein, ostensibly in exchange for religious education and protection.) The town of Ollantaytambo remained inhabited throughout this period of tumult. Today, its oldest pre-Columbian dwellings share space with Spanish colonial buildings and contemporary constructions.

title: Servant spaces over kitchen extension of ca. 1800; walls raised
headline: Poplar Grove, Poplar Grove Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Built early 1760s as a 2-story, hall/parlor house and a attached kitchen wing. Kitchen wing converted to a dining room and teh wing extended with a new kitchen extension later in the century. Around 1818, the house is remodeled, a passage inserted in the hall, and a new stair wing is added to the rear. Soon thereafter, the stair wing is expanded to include a new formal dining room. At the same time or not long afterwards, a 1 1/2 story frame shed is built off the back of the main house against the stair/dining room wing. In the 1860s, teh stair was rebuilt and some mantels were replaced. A small brick wing was cconstructed on the end of the house and the house was given a yellow wash. Then, in the early 1880s, the seccond story was raised taller (lifting the original roof frame, including the shingle lath and flooring) and a steep cross gable was added to the front, the kitchen was raised in similar fashion, and a single-story farm office wing was added to the opposite gable, complete with its own steep cross gable. Perhaps at this same time the dining room wing and the chamber above it were expanded with a polygonal end. A frame stair passage was built onto the end of the 1860s brick wing.
creator: Willie Graham

description: Plantation of miller, planter, businessman, William Massie and his wife, Maria. This is the site of the first commercial orchard in Nelson County. Massie and his wife bred slaves and kept detailed accounts, with the last birth recorded in 1866 by his wife, since he had since died.. At the time of William Massies death in 1862, he held 170 enslaved people and owned about 2000 acres. The main house was constructed in 1814; the brickkitchen/laundry/hostpital for birthing, and quarter was constructed sometime between 1834-36. Double-pen log quarter (saddleback); pit-sawn joists; wrought nails used in doors. Wooden hinges.

Gable-front building that measures about 13' by 32', with deep overhanginging eaves and early sheds, both on three sides.Set on wood piers (now replaced) and covered with wooden shingles. title: Stairs in entry, 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Ash dump in Scotch oven; 1765
headline: Whitehall, 1915 Whitehall Road, Annapolis, Anne Arundel County, Maryland
description: Gov. Horatio Sharpe built Whitehall as a party or banqueting house in the 1760s. Some original design drawings survive, possibly from the hands of Joseph Horatio Anderson who later undertook construction of the Maryland State House. These drawings show the house partially as built but also with features that were not realized until the 20th century. Anderson drafted an octagonal stable, which was reportedly erected.  Dendro (Worhington) for the original build dates to the winter of 1764/65. That work included the main block, with a central cubed room flanked by one story wings. All three rooms were intended as entertaining spaces. The central hall has a vaulted ceiling with elaborate rococo plasterwork and heavily carved woodwork around the walls, doors, and windows. Some of the applied carvings to the walls are original, but heavily repaired in the 1950s. Plasterwork in the cove ceiling was also restored and elaborated at this time. (I presume the central ceiling medallion was wholly made up in the 1950s and that the original treatment is long lost.) The center hall has exquisite, full-length yellow pine flooring and no evidence of carpeting. The flanking rooms probably served as dining room and a parlor-like space. Both are heated, have plaster cornices, and have heavily carved woodwork. These rooms received several generations of carpeting. Since the quality of their flooring was less than the hall, they might have been fitted with them from the outset. The house was intended for entertainments and little accommodation was made for overnight guests, giving the structure a banqueting house like feel, albeit it without cellar service spaces (where the kitchen was placed is unknown for this original construction.) A basement story was created underneath with a passage and two vaulted rooms (probably at least one used for wet storage). Flanking them are two private family chambers. title: Attic window; 1790s
headline: Bordligton, 200 Bordlington Farm Lane, Centreville, Queen Anne's County, Maryland
description: Orlando Ridout thought the house was built about 1780; Carl Lounsbury is skeptical that it would have been built during the War. The house does show on the 1798 Direct Tax; conceivably it was built erlier in that decade. The mantels, for instance, appear too delicate to be much earlier. The use of crossettes on the frontispiece and two surviving mantels, while a bit old fashioned, show on otehr 1790s houses in the county. Seemingly the side of the house facing away from the water was considered the front--it is laid in Flemish bond without any glazing, while the rear elevation is laid in all glazed header Flemish bond. If the house dates to the 1790s, the choice of using this bond pattern on the rear was to create a very modern front without it and yet use the old fashioned treatment on the back--much like how the houses in Chestertown of the 1780s and '90s were treated.
creator: Willie Graham

title: Double quarter, ca. 1840
headline: Hyde Park Slave Quarter, 6808 West Courthouse Road, Burkeville, Nottoway County, Virginia
description: National Register Nomination for Hyde Park suggests the house was likely built between 1762 and 1782 by John Fowlkes. This includes the central portion of the house built with English bond cellar walls and Flemish above. The house was significantly rebuilt and retrimmed around 1840 possibly by Paschal J. Fowlkes. It was purchased in 1938 by Richmond department store owner William B. Thalhimer who used it as a training farm for Jewish students from Nazi Germany of the German agricultural Gross Breesen Institute. Two buildings sat to the rear of the house. One recently collapsed, leaving standing its two brick end chimneys. The Thalhimers referred to that building as the overseers' house, although it likely served as a kitchen and service building, with quarters upstairs--possibly for the overseer. A second structure remains standing that likely served as a double slave quarter. It is of frame construction and consists two rooms with a center chimney. Each room has its own, front exterior door. A tight winder stair rose in the front corner of the right room to an unheated and unfinished attic accessed only from the one side. The interior of the quarter appears to have had no finish until the 20th century. The frame sits on stone piers. The chimney is constructed of stone and includes large cooking fireplaces in each room. They are each spanned by a large oak lintel beveled on its rear and a wooden trammel bar that ran the width of the fireplace, set into its flue. Built of hewn and pit sawn framing and with mature, machine-cut nails. Conceivably it was constructed around 1840 when the house was first improved. The structure has been assumed to have been constructed as the kitchen and laundry for the site, but that seems unlikely.
creator: Willie Graham
categories: talheimer,hyde park,quarter,slave quarter,slave,enslaved,work,double quarter,slave house,timber frame,center chimney

title: Salvaged trim with trace of a dovetail joint
headline: Melwood, 11008 Old Marlboro Pike, Upper Marlboro, Prince George's County, Maryland
description: First-period construction of the house was dated by dendrochronology to 1713 and spring of 1714. It was owned by Henry Darnell Jr. (1682–1737) who also owned an adjacent 7,000-acre plantation granted under the name His Lordship's Kindness. This was a timber-frame, hall-and-parlor house with a rear extension. It was lengthened slightly in the 1740s or '50s. Dendrochronology shows that additional changes were  changes were made  in 1767 and 1768 (felling dates of the trees), which included bricking in the front, gables, and portions of the rear, lengthening of the house, raising the front to two stories, retrimming the interior, inserting a center passage, presumably rebuilding the rear wing, and reusing timbers on the front side of the roof as it was jacked up to two stories, but retaining the single-story slope and framing of the rear. Salvaged timber from the house with traces of a dovetailed joint.
creator: Willie Graham
